learn.title: Solid-State Batteries: Safer Power
learn.intro: Solid-state batteries replace liquid electrolytes with solid materials, reducing fire risks and enabling higher energy storage. They’re crucial for EVs and grid storage, offering 2-3x energy density of lithium-ion. This tech could cut transportation emissions by 70% if widely adopted by 2030.
learn.c1_text: Unlike liquid electrolytes, solid materials like ceramics or polymers prevent leaks and dendrite formation. For example, a solid-state battery in an EV could store 500 Wh/kg vs. 250 Wh/kg for lithium-ion. This means smaller, lighter batteries for the same range, reducing material costs by up to 40%.
learn.c2_text: Higher energy density allows longer drives or grid storage in compact spaces. A solid-state battery pack could power a home for 10 hours vs. 6 hours with lithium-ion. Toyota plans to deploy this tech in EVs by 2025, targeting a 30% reduction in charging frequency.
learn.c3_text: India’s push for EVs could benefit from solid-state batteries. With 50% of new cars expected to be electric by 2030, these batteries could extend EV range by 50% while cutting fire risks. The Indian government’s $1B EV fund may prioritize this tech for safer, cost-effective solutions.
learn.stat2: Solid-state batteries: 500 Wh/kg energy density (US DOE)
learn.stat3: Lithium-ion risk: 1 in 10 million fire incidents (US DOE)

learn.title: Lithium-Ion Grids: Storing Solar Power
learn.intro: Lithium-ion batteries store excess solar energy for use when the sun isn’t shining. They stabilize grids, prevent blackouts, and enable 24/7 renewable energy. India’s solar farms use them to cut reliance on coal by 15% annually.
learn.c1_text: These batteries store energy like a bank account. During peak solar hours, they charge; at night, they discharge. A 100 kWh system can power 1,000 homes for 1 hour. They’re 90% efficient, losing only 10% energy during cycles.
learn.c2_text: They balance supply-demand fluctuations. In California, lithium-ion grids reduced outages by 40% during heatwaves. By storing 10 MW, they can support 10,000 homes for 15 minutes, acting as a backup during solar dips.
learn.c3_text: India’s 60 GW solar target uses lithium-ion storage to manage intermittency. The MNRE mandates 5% storage in new solar projects. By 2025, this could reduce coal-based power by 20 GW, saving 30 million tons of CO2 yearly.

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells: Clean Vehicle Power
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells split water into hydrogen and oxygen, generating electricity with only water vapor as waste. They power buses and trucks with zero emissions. India’s hydrogen buses emit 90% less CO2 than diesel counterparts.
learn.c1_text: Water is split into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable energy. A 1 kg hydrogen tank can power a car for 600 km. The process is 70% efficient, and hydrogen can be stored safely in tanks at 700 bar pressure.
learn.c2_text: Fuel cells combine hydrogen with oxygen to create electricity, emitting only water. A hydrogen bus in Delhi saves 5 tons of CO2 yearly. Refueling takes 5 minutes, faster than charging EVs, making it ideal for long-haul transport.
learn.c3_text: India plans 500 hydrogen fueling stations by 2030. The National Hydrogen Mission targets 5 million fuel cell vehicles. Delhi’s hydrogen buses have cut particulate matter by 40% in pilot zones, improving air quality.
learn.stat1: India: 5,000 fuel cell vehicles target by 2030

learn.title: Rooftop Solar in India: Net Metering
learn.intro: Rooftop solar panels on Indian homes generate electricity for personal use or sale. Net metering lets owners sell excess power to the grid, reducing bills by 30-50%. Over 10,000 MW of rooftop solar is installed in India, cutting coal use.
learn.c1_text: Panels convert sunlight into DC electricity. A 5 kW system in Delhi produces 6,000 kWh/year. At ₹8 per kWh, this offsets ₹48,000 in annual bills, making solar cost-effective for middle-class homes.
learn.c2_text: Excess power is fed to the grid, crediting the owner. In Tamil Nadu, a 10 kW system earns ₹12,000/year via net metering. This incentivizes adoption, with 40% of urban rooftops now having solar panels.
learn.c3_text: MNRE’s 2023 policy targets 40 GW rooftop solar by 2026. Net metering reduced diesel generator use in cities by 15%. A Bengaluru factory saved ₹2.5 million/year by combining rooftop solar with net metering.

learn.title: Sodium-Ion Batteries: Cheaper Storage
learn.intro: Sodium-ion batteries use abundant sodium instead of lithium, cutting costs by 30-50%. They’re ideal for grid storage in India, where lithium prices are volatile. IRENA reports they could power 30% of Indian grids by 2030.
learn.c1_text: Sodium is 500x more common than lithium. A 100 kWh sodium-ion battery costs ₹2 million vs. ₹3.5 million for lithium-ion. This makes them viable for large-scale storage in India’s thermal power plants.
learn.c2_text: They store 150 Wh/kg vs. 250 Wh/kg for lithium-ion. However, they’re safer and cheaper. India’s Tamil Nadu grid uses them to store 500 MW of solar energy, reducing peak demand costs by 20%.
learn.c3_text: IRENA’s report highlights sodium-ion adoption in India’s renewable grids. By 2030, 20 GW of storage could be sodium-ion-based, saving ₹50 billion in lithium costs. They’re ideal for balancing India’s 140 GW renewable target.

learn.title: Solar PV: Sunlight to Power
learn.intro: Solar PV panels convert sunlight into electricity via the photoelectric effect. A single panel can power a home for hours. India’s solar capacity grew 20% yearly, with 70 GW installed in 2024, making it the third-largest solar market.
learn.c1_text: Photons from sunlight knock electrons loose in silicon cells, creating current. A 300 W panel generates 1.5 kWh/day in Rajasthan. At peak sun, it produces 1 kW instantly, enough to charge a phone in minutes.
learn.c2_text: Panels are 15-20% efficient, meaning 20% of sunlight becomes electricity. A 5 kW system in Gujarat produces 7,000 kWh/year. India’s 70 GW capacity avoids 100 million tons of CO2 annually.
learn.c3_text: MNRE reports 20% annual growth in solar installations. Net metering and subsidies have made rooftop solar affordable. A Mumbai office saved ₹1.2 million/year by switching to solar, reducing grid dependence by 70%.
